text: Currently, most video streaming over Internet is implemented with a new technology named HTTP-based adaptive streaming (HAS). This technology has two aspects: 1) riding on top of HTTP/TCP for firewall/NATS traversal and bandwidth sharing; and 2) using pre-encoded videos with multiple discrete bitrates for scalable video bitrate adaptivity while keeping the resource-demanding video encoding out of the feedback loop. It is a common belief that the client's observed TCP throughput can be used as the signal for the available bandwidth which is then used to guide the decision for the video bitrate. In this paper, we investigate this belief and show that it can be invalid for current Internet when HAS becomes one of the dominant traffics on the Internet. In case of a cross point where multiple HAS clients share the same outgoing link, and given the fact that every client is empowered to choose any of the bitrates available in the stream (which are usually discretely distributed) from its server, the other sharing clients and the discrete choice of bitrates at each client's side confuses the client about what could be its fair-share bandwidth for the shared link. Experiments verify the drawbacks of the current HAS client implementation, and we further demonstrate the necessity of an improved mechanism in this case. We propose an "probe-and-adapt" solution at the application layer similar to, yet complementary to, the transport-layer network rate adaptation provided by TCP. We implement aHAS client side algorithm PANDA (Probe-And-Adapt-for-Network-Dispersions-and-Async) to demonstrate the merits of such application-layer adaptation for current HAS implementation. The extensive experiments carried out with our testbed confirm the reduced video rate instability achieved with PANDA as well as the acceptability of the increased risk of buffer underrun (such as stalling for 1-5 seconds) at the cost of this improvement. The improved client-side rate adaptation forHAS reduces video rate oscillation by up to 60% at the expense of up to 5% more chance of a 1-5 seconds-long buffer underrun event.
explanation: Reads like a compact research abstract with plausible technical content. The frequent use of invented acronyms (PANDA, aHAS) and some awkward phrasing can indicate AI generation.
